med_asym = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df4_sm[df4_sm.group .== g, :asym])) for g in GROUP_ORDER) med_q = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df4_sm[df4_sm.group .== g, :qfactor])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)
st_asym_hfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["asym HF-AB"] st_asym_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["asym LF-AB"] st_q_hfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["qfactor HF-AB"] st_q_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["qfactor LF-AB"] st_q_hflf = fig4_stats.matched_diff["qfactor HF-LF"] st_dir_hf = fig4_stats.within_group["dir_bias HF"] st_dir_lf = fig4_stats.within_group["dir_bias LF"] st_npk_hfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["npk_inflow_asym HF-AB"] st_npk_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["npk_inflow_asym LF-AB"] st_npkbias_hf = fig4_stats.within_group["npk_bias HF"] st_npkbias_lf = fig4_stats.within_group["npk_bias LF"] st_npkbias_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["npk_bias LF-AB"] st_global_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["global_pair_asym LF-AB"]
println(""" In healthy walking, the two legs influence each other roughly symmetrically -- perturbations on one side propagate to the other with similar strength in both directions. Stroke disrupts this balance. We quantify inter-limb coupling asymmetry as the ratio between the stronger and weaker cross-body influence directions (a ratio of 1 means perfect symmetry). This ratio rises monotonically from AB (median $(round(med_asym["AB"]; digits=2))) through HF ($(round(med_asym["HF"]; digits=2))) to LF ($(round(med_asym["LF"]; digits=2))), indicating progressively one-sided coupling between the legs. Hierarchical bootstrap confirms larger asymmetry in both stroke groups relative to AB (HF--AB \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_asym_hfab.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_asym_hfab.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_asym_hfab.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_asym_hfab.p)); LF--AB \$\\Delta\$ = $(round(st_asym_lfab.point; digits=2)), [$(round(st_asym_lfab.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_asym_lfab.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_asym_lfab.p))). Moreover, in LF gait the dominant direction of influence runs from the paretic to the non-paretic side (mean log-ratio = $(round(st_dir_lf.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_dir_lf.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_dir_lf.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_dir_lf.p))), with HF trending similarly but less certain ($(round(st_dir_hf.point; digits=2)), [$(round(st_dir_hf.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_dir_hf.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_dir_hf.p))). In other words, the impaired limb's dynamics propagate forcefully across the body, and the intact side must absorb and accommodate those perturbations.
How narrowly tuned is each group's coordination to the stride frequency? The Q-factor measures this: higher Q means the system responds strongly at stride frequency but poorly at neighbouring frequencies. HF gait is the most sharply tuned (median Q = $(round(med_q["HF"]; digits=1)) vs. AB $(round(med_q["AB"]; digits=1)) and LF $(round(med_q["LF"]; digits=1))), consistent with a coordination strategy that locks onto the stride rhythm but sacrifices adaptability to perturbations at other frequencies. Hierarchical bootstrap confirms higher Q in HF than AB (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_q_hfab.point; digits=1)), 95% CI [$(round(st_q_hfab.ci[1]; digits=1)), $(round(st_q_hfab.ci[2]; digits=1))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_q_hfab.p))) and in LF than AB (\$\\Delta\$ = $(round(st_q_lfab.point; digits=1)), [$(round(st_q_lfab.ci[1]; digits=1)), $(round(st_q_lfab.ci[2]; digits=1))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_q_lfab.p))), while the HF--LF contrast is less certain (\$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_q_hflf.p))). LF gait thus combines high spatial asymmetry with moderately narrow tuning -- a coordination pattern that is both directionally biased and somewhat rigid.
To localise where in the kinematic chain inter-limb asymmetry is concentrated, we examine hip-to-knee coupling. In AB gait, the hips drive both knees roughly equally. In both stroke groups, this coupling shifts toward the non-paretic knee: the log-ratio of hip-to-non-paretic-knee vs. hip-to-paretic-knee coupling is near zero in AB but positive in stroke (HF--AB \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_npk_hfab.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_npk_hfab.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_npk_hfab.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npk_hfab.p)); LF--AB \$\\Delta\$ = $(round(st_npk_lfab.point; digits=2)), [$(round(st_npk_lfab.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_npk_lfab.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npk_lfab.p))). Both stroke groups also show strong directional bias *into* the non-paretic knee from the hips (HF: mean = $(round(st_npkbias_hf.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_npkbias_hf.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_npkbias_hf.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npkbias_hf.p)); LF: mean = $(round(st_npkbias_lf.point; digits=2)), [$(round(st_npkbias_lf.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_npkbias_lf.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npkbias_lf.p))), with LF exceeding AB (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_npkbias_lfab.point; digits=2)), [$(round(st_npkbias_lfab.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_npkbias_lfab.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npkbias_lfab.p))). A broader summary of all pairwise joint asymmetries trends upward in LF relative to AB (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_global_lfab.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_global_lfab.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_global_lfab.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_global_lfab.p))), but the clearest finding is that stroke-related coupling asymmetry is concentrated specifically around the non-paretic knee rather than being diffusely spread across all joints. The intact leg does not simply "compensate" in a general sense -- the model reveals a specific pathway through which the non-paretic knee absorbs and redistributes perturbations originating from the impaired side. """)
sec.p: In healthy walking, the two legs influence each other roughly symmetrically – perturbations on one side propagate to the other with similar strength in both directions. Stroke disrupts this balance. We quantify inter-limb coupling asymmetry as the ratio between the stronger and weaker cross-body influence directions (a ratio of 1 means perfect symmetry). This ratio rises monotonically from AB (median 1.23) through HF (1.69) to LF (1.89), indicating progressively one-sided coupling between the legs. Hierarchical bootstrap confirms larger asymmetry in both stroke groups relative to AB (HF–AB \Delta Δmedian = 0.47, 95% CI [0.24, 0.92], p p = 0.002; LF–AB \Delta Δ = 0.66, [0.28, 1.75], p p < 0.001). Moreover, in LF gait the dominant direction of influence runs from the paretic to the non-paretic side (mean log-ratio = 0.6, 95% CI [0.28, 0.9], p p < 0.001), with HF trending similarly but less certain (0.21, [-0.01, 0.45], p p = 0.062). In other words, the impaired limb’s dynamics propagate forcefully across the body, and the intact side must absorb and accommodate those perturbations.

How narrowly tuned is each group’s coordination to the stride frequency? The Q-factor measures this: higher Q means the system responds strongly at stride frequency but poorly at neighbouring frequencies. HF gait is the most sharply tuned (median Q = 69.6 vs. AB 14.3 and LF 31.3), consistent with a coordination strategy that locks onto the stride rhythm but sacrifices adaptability to perturbations at other frequencies. Hierarchical bootstrap confirms higher Q in HF than AB ( \Delta Δmedian = 55.3, 95% CI [24.6, 93.5], p p < 0.001) and in LF than AB ( \Delta Δ = 17.1, [2.5, 59.6], p p = 0.012), while the HF–LF contrast is less certain ( p p = 0.176). LF gait thus combines high spatial asymmetry with moderately narrow tuning – a coordination pattern that is both directionally biased and somewhat rigid.

To localise where in the kinematic chain inter-limb asymmetry is concentrated, we examine hip-to-knee coupling. In AB gait, the hips drive both knees roughly equally. In both stroke groups, this coupling shifts toward the non-paretic knee: the log-ratio of hip-to-non-paretic-knee vs. hip-to-paretic-knee coupling is near zero in AB but positive in stroke (HF–AB
\Delta Δmedian = 0.23, 95% CI [0.01, 0.65], p p = 0.034; LF–AB \Delta Δ = 0.79, [0.12, 1.39], p p = 0.016). Both stroke groups also show strong directional bias into the non-paretic knee from the hips (HF: mean = 1.09, 95% CI [0.82, 1.36], p p < 0.001; LF: mean = 1.19, [0.87, 1.48],
p p < 0.001), with LF exceeding AB ( \Delta Δmedian = 0.83, [0.18, 1.33], p p = 0.012). A broader summary of all pairwise joint asymmetries trends upward in LF relative to AB ( \Delta Δmedian = 0.33, 95% CI [-0.02, 0.57], p p = 0.056), but the clearest finding is that stroke-related coupling asymmetry is concentrated specifically around the non-paretic knee rather than being diffusely spread across all joints. The intact leg does not simply “compensate” in a general sense – the model reveals a specific pathway through which the non-paretic knee absorbs and redistributes perturbations originating from the impaired side.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Three operator-level signatures emerge from delay-embedded DMD of 299 gait trials, collectively revealing that stroke reorganises–rather than merely degrades–the transient dynamical structure of locomotion.

Speed-dependent non-normality. Able-bodied gait maintains a stable, speed-invariant level of non-normality, indicating that the transient complexity of the locomotor operator is robust across operating points. Both stroke groups show elevated speed-dependence ( r = 0.35 r=0.35 HF, r = 0.47 r=0.47 LF vs. r = 0.11 r=0.11 AB), meaning that their capacity for transient amplification is fragile–contingent on walking at particular speeds. As discussed in the Introduction, all three analysis frameworks–Floquet multipliers, Lyapunov exponents, and DMD–fit autonomous models to a system that is continuously driven and controlled (Dingwell and Kang 2007; Ting and McKay 2007). The maximum Lyapunov exponent
\lambda_S λS remains the best-validated predictor of fall risk (Bruijn et al. 2013), but as a scalar divergence rate of an autonomous reconstruction it cannot distinguish intrinsic instability from neural driving, nor can it capture the directional or frequency-dependent structure of transient dynamics. The Henrici departure, by indexing how severely the autonomous linear model must distort its eigenvector geometry to fit the driven, nonlinear data, captures a complementary dimension of locomotor organisation. Where
\lambda_S λS summarises the dominant instability direction, the resolvent structure maps the full landscape of transient amplification across frequencies and spatial channels. The speed-dependence of this structure in stroke gait–absent in able-bodied gait–suggests that the model-reality gap (i.e., how “non-autonomous” or “nonlinear” the true system is relative to the linear surrogate) varies with operating point in impaired but not in healthy locomotion.

Spectral organisation: eigenvalue proximity vs. pseudospectral spreading. The harmonic resolvent profiles initially appear paradoxical: HF has the highest Henrici departure yet the steepest normalised harmonic fall-off, while LF has the lowest non-normality yet a normalised profile similar to AB. The resolution lies in recognising that two distinct mechanisms shape the resolvent–eigenvalue proximity to the stride frequency (which sets absolute gain at each harmonic) and pseudospectral spreading via the operator’s condition number (which determines how broadly gain extends away from eigenvalue locations). HF operators park an eigenvalue very close to the stride fundamental, producing massive h1 gain. But HF’s condition number ( \kappa \approx 1{,}200 κ≈1,200) is an order of magnitude below AB’s ( \kappa \approx 18{,}700 κ≈18,700), so the pseudospectral halo that would spread this gain to neighbouring harmonics is narrower. The steep normalised fall-off in HF therefore reflects disproportionate inflation of the fundamental, not suppression of higher harmonics–absolute gains at h2–h5 are comparable to or exceed AB’s. Conversely, LF operators are nearly well-conditioned ( \kappa \approx 46 κ≈46), meaning almost no pseudospectral spreading; their flat normalised profile arises simply because no single eigenvalue dominates the stride frequency. AB combines extreme ill-conditioning with moderate eigenvalue–stride coupling to produce both high absolute gain and broad bandwidth–the pseudospectral effects sustain resolvent gain at frequencies far from any eigenvalue. From the perspective of Wu, Brunton, and Revzen (2021), the condition number gradient across groups reflects how differently the Koopman operator’s non-normal structure is distorted by neurological impairment. The non-normality they identify as a challenge for DMD convergence here becomes a graded diagnostic: AB dynamics strain the autonomous linear model broadly across the spectrum; HF dynamics strain it acutely at one frequency; LF dynamics strain it least of all.

Asymmetric coupling focused on the non-paretic knee. The directionality of inter-limb coupling provides perhaps the most clinically informative signature. At the coarse inter-limb level, LF shows clear paretic-dominant cross-body drive, with HF trending in the same direction. At finer joint resolution, however, the most robust effect is even more specific: hip-knee coupling becomes concentrated on the non-paretic knee, and both stroke groups show strong directional bias into that knee from the hips. Rather than the intact side simply compensating for the impaired side in a diffuse manner, the operator structure suggests that stroke channels transient coupling through a knee-centered pathway on the non-paretic limb. That picture remains consistent with the clinical observation that the “good” leg works harder during stroke gait, but it localises the asymmetry to a specific operator-level route through which perturbations are absorbed and redistributed.

Relationship to existing stability measures. Bruijn et al. (2013) evaluated gait stability measures against four validity criteria: construct validity, convergent validity, predictive validity, and discriminant validity. Floquet multipliers showed poor construct validity for human gait–not only because strict periodicity is violated, but because they require prohibitively large datasets (>10,000 strides in Revzen and Guckenheimer (2008) vs. ~15–50 strides in typical human trials), demand a reliable phase estimator (Revzen and Guckenheimer 2008, 2012), and are acutely noise-sensitive. \lambda_S λS showed good predictive validity but limited discriminant validity between populations, and as Dingwell and Kang (2007) demonstrated, the coexistence of positive
\lambda_S λS (local divergence) with orbital stability reflects the driven nature of the system rather than a genuine autonomous instability. The operator-level metrics introduced here show substantial discriminant validity, with the strongest and most consistent separations in AB-vs-stroke contrasts, and capture facets of locomotor organisation–transient richness, frequency bandwidth, directional coupling–that no single scalar can represent. Like DMD itself, these metrics inherit the autonomous-model assumption, but they make the strain of that assumption visible: non-normality is high precisely where the linear autonomous model must work hardest to approximate driven, nonlinear dynamics. Establishing their predictive validity for fall risk and their convergent validity with
\lambda_S λS are natural directions for future work.

Methodological considerations. Our approach builds on the DMD framework of Schmid (2010) and Tu et al. (2014), with delay embedding following Takens (1981). Wu, Brunton, and Revzen (2021) showed that DMD applied to limit-cycle data faces challenges from Krylov space closure and non-normality-amplified estimation errors, and characterised performance via a dataset metric (number of initial conditions \times × trajectory length). Our dataset of 299 trials with 1500 time points each provides substantial coverage; the stability enforcement (eigenvalue projection inside the unit disk) and SVD truncation (retaining >99% variance) guard against the most severe numerical pathologies they identify. Nevertheless, several limitations apply. The analysis uses a single treadmill dataset; overground walking may produce different operator structure. The choice of embedding lag ( \tau = 10 τ=10) is motivated by stride-period considerations at 100 Hz but has not been exhaustively optimised. The linear operator is a surrogate–it captures the Koopman-linearised dynamics faithfully only within the delay-embedded subspace, and nonlinear effects beyond this approximation are not modelled.

Clinical implications. Collectively, these results suggest that post-stroke locomotor impairment is not a scalar degradation but a structured reorganisation of transient dynamics. Restoring walking speed or step symmetry may not restore the non-normal richness that characterises healthy gait. The group-specific signatures identified here–fragile non-normality, narrowed bandwidth in HF, and knee-centered asymmetry that is strongest in LF–point toward different rehabilitation targets for different functional levels. Interventions that broaden the transient repertoire, such as perturbation-based balance training, multi-frequency rhythmic cueing, or assistive devices that modulate inter-limb coupling directionality, may prove more effective than approaches targeting kinematic outcomes alone.

Outlook. The present work demonstrates that non-normality in data-driven linear operators is not merely a numerical nuisance but a window into the transient organisation of biological movement. Future extensions–incorporating residual DMD for rigorous spectral convergence guarantees (Colbrook, Ayton, and Szöke 2023), measure-theoretic Koopman formulations (Mezić 2005, 2013), or hybrid embeddings that combine delay coordinates with nonlinear observables–promise richer operator-level phenotypes for clinical stratification and closed-loop control.
